title: Reverse burden of proof under NDPS - the constitutional challenge
p1:
In NDPS cases, the rules of criminal law flip in a way most people don’t expect.
Instead of the State proving guilt first, the accused often has to prove innocence.
p2:
Most people assume criminal law always works one way: prosecution must prove guilt beyond reasonable doubt.
But under the NDPS Act, Sections 35 and 54 create a presumption once possession is shown.
That means the burden can shift to the accused to explain how they were not aware of the contraband.
Many first-time readers think, “I didn’t know that this was even possible.”
p3:
Once contraband is recovered from what the law calls “conscious possession,” the court presumes a guilty mental state.
The accused then has to rebut this presumption with evidence or a believable explanation.
This creates a real constitutional tension with the idea of presumption of innocence under Article 21.
Courts have upheld it, but with strict safeguards to prevent misuse.
Practically, small details like ownership, access, and custody become critical in defence strategy.
conclusion: Do you think reversing the burden of proof in NDPS cases strengthens enforcement or risks undermining the presumption of innocence?
